UX Research · Information Architecture · Mobile App · AR

Green Guardian —
UX Case Study

An augmented reality eCommerce mobile app for indoor plant parents — designed end-to-end from user research and synthesis through to information architecture, UX design, brand identity, and logo development. Green Guardian allows users to browse a plant catalogue, visualise plants in their homes via AR, and purchase and care for them through a single app.

Role

UX / UI Designer

Tools

Figma · Sketch · Illustrator · InVision · FlowMapp

Timeline

3 weeks — Nov 2023

Scope

UX Research UX / UI Mobile · AR
Research→
Synthesis→
Goals & Features→
Architecture→
UX Design→
Branding

01 — The Proposal

How might we help plant parents
feel confident at home?

The indoor plant market has grown significantly as more people look to bring nature into their living spaces — but the experience of buying, placing, and caring for plants remains riddled with uncertainty. Most plant owners learn by failure rather than guidance, and no existing app brings together visualization, purchasing, and care into a single cohesive experience.

The Challenge

How might we advise users to visualise and design their living spaces while helping plant parents feel confident in their tending and care of indoor plants?

Key Objective

Green Guardian should enable users to select indoor plants from a catalogue and virtually curate their homes through AR. After visualising plants, users should be able to purchase and learn how to care for them — all within the same app.

02 — The Solution

From research to AR —
a holistic design approach

Green Guardian was designed to allow users to browse a catalogue of indoor plants and place them in their homes via augmented reality. The app also includes Green Guidance — a feature that optimises plant growth stages, sunlight expectations, and seasonal suggestions based on the user's geographic location.

The project was approached holistically: branding, logo exploration, marketing considerations, and UX design were all developed in parallel, ensuring the product's visual identity was as thoughtfully considered as its functionality.

03 — Research

Understanding the indoor plant
market and its people

Green Guardian began with ideation, market research, and competitive analysis. I drafted two research proposals covering both secondary and primary research — drawing on professional experience with battle cards, market positioning, SWOT analysis, and competitor evaluation for the secondary phase, and in-depth in-person interviews and observational inquiry for primary research.

Research Goal: To develop an understanding of the indoor plant market, identify the target demographic's behaviour when using plant-tending applications, and understand the successes and failures of competitor apps.

Key finding: There is considerable potential in the AR indoor plant conceptualisation market within eCommerce — but the right questions haven't yet been asked, and not enough problems solved, within this growing space.
Research & Competitive Analysis

Market analysis, competitive landscape, and research findings.

Interview Findings — Participants aged 25–30, all with plant-buying experience

"You don't really know what you're getting into when you're buying a plant."

— Participant from Den Bosch

"Shopping in person can be overwhelming and really time consuming. I need a centralised app that has a catalogue of all the plants at home and automates and alerts me when I need to care for them."

— Participant from Enschede

"If it meets the requirements for the exact space I'm looking for on the label I'll buy it. Houseplants are more about design."

— Participant from Deventer

🌱

Needs

  • Centralised hub with automated care alerts
  • Plants that dimensionally fit the living space
  • Ability to order plants online after visual assessment
⚡

Pains

  • No centralised information hub for plant care basics
  • Having to "hope for the best" or "learn by failure"
  • In-person shopping is overwhelming and style-agnostic
✨

Desires

  • A living space accented aesthetically by indoor plants
  • Plant care automation with "learning as you go"
  • Confidence and knowledge around purchases and care

04 — Synthesis

From data to a person —
the Empathy Map & Persona

After analysing the data, findings were synthesised into reference points beginning with an empathy map — created from interviews, secondary research, and observational analysis. This helped contextualise future users' encounters with Green Guardian and informed demographic and psychographic decisions that shaped the persona, brand, and overall design direction.

A user persona was formed strictly from research and interview quotes, allowing the ability to mould a representational character whose journey through plant tending could guide every subsequent design decision.

Empathy map synthesising interview data, observational research, and secondary findings.

User persona constructed strictly from research — representing the target audience's relationship with plant tending and technology.

05 — Goals & Features

Aligning user needs with
business objectives

Before defining features, three goal categories were mapped — user goals, business goals, and common goals — to ensure every product decision served both the person using the app and the business building it.

👤 User Goals

  • A living space that feels natural and inviting
  • Confidence in plant care and tending
  • Simple learning curve into the hobby
  • Know all details before buying — no risk of failure
  • Plant care and scheduling automation
  • Estimate dimensions in living space via AR

💼 Business Goals

  • Become an industry leader in the plant app category
  • Create new logo, branding, and UI to position in market
  • Optimise plant growing for every indoor hobbyist
  • Sell products through external vendors
  • Keep product information and cataloguing up to date
  • Recommend plants to users

🙏 Common Goals

  • A delightful plant-tending and finding experience
  • Stoke and develop a new plant-tending hobby
  • Catalogue and information of products
  • Order and receive new plants for living space
  • See plants in live AR before purchasing

Product Feature Priorities

Ordered by importance to user goals

01

Explore Catalogue — Filter and browse plants and categories. Favourite, add to My Plants, and access detailed care and conditions information.

02

AR Visualisation — Place plants in the home via AR, estimate sunlight, place multiple plants, and seamlessly flow into purchases from the AR view.

03

My Profile — High-level plant involvement, living spaces, recommendations, and purchase history. Gateway to My Plants, Care Schedule, and Notifications.

04

My Plants — Plants categorised by Living Space, with recommendations, plant profiles, and care notifications for each.

05

Plant Profile — Detailed individual plant information, AR visualisation, purchase flow, add to My Plants, and Care setup.

06

Care Schedule — Set up, view, and manage care for plants with push notifications and iCal/Google Calendar integration.

07

Order Plant Flow — Integrated into AR Visualisation, Recommended Plants, and Plant Profile — a seamless path from discovery to purchase.

06 — Architecture

How Might We — turning goals
into design problems

To create the physical product structure, I first acknowledged business aims and necessary features for user goals, then formulated a main architectural plan through "How Might We" statements. These allowed the ability to meet user needs while considering the tasks and mappings of potential technology — grounding every structural decision in a real user problem.

"How might we help first-time plant owners feel confident in their tending and care?"

"How might we create for users a more accessible and usable plant buying experience?"

"How might we help plant hobbyists visualise and design their living spaces?"

07 — User Flows & Sitemap

Mapping the paths users
will actually take

From the architectural goals, I identified tasks and user flows that would meet the persona's needs, desires, and goals within the context of her life journey. These flows uncovered an application sitemap providing the most practical route to those goals. I designed miniature wireframes in FlowMapp to illustrate potential screen solutions before moving into UX design — leaving room for iteration throughout.

User flow & task flow (side by side) — followed by the full sitemap showing every screen and connection in the app.

08 — UX Design

From sketches to wireframes —
rapid, iterative prototyping

After researching and analysing design patterns that fit the information architecture, business goals, and user needs, I sketched solutions. Using rapid prototyping and Marvel to move ideas forward, I focused on meeting the How Might We goals in an iterative process — ensuring WCAG 2.0 and Apple Human Interface Guidelines were met throughout, from sketches through to wireframing via Sketch and Marvel.

Low-fidelity wireframes exploring interaction patterns, screen flows, and layout decisions.

09 — Branding & Visual Identity

A brand rooted in the
nurturing essence of plants

Branding was developed in parallel with UX — not as an afterthought but as an integral part of the product design process. The colour palette converges to define the app's unique identity and evoke the calm, natural emotion that plant care should inspire. The logo exploration centred on simplicity: rooted in the nurturing essence of plant care, the final mark is clean, organic, and immediately recognisable.

Brand Identity & Colour Palette

Colour palette and brand identity — where colours converge to define the app's unique identity and evoke emotion.

Final Logo & Brand Story

App logo exploration and final mark — rooted in simplicity, symbolising the nurturing essence of plant care.

Project reflection

"In this project, I undertook a comprehensive approach — user research, synthesis, architectural planning, and UX design — alongside crafting a brand story and distinctive logo. The next phase will focus on presenting the refined interfaces designed to optimise user engagement within the app."
